rationale: The citing paper uses Manchanda et al. (2006) in a specific argumentative role, not just as a generic literature list entry. Rao cites it to support the claim that click-throughs may be a weak proxy for actual purchasing, which directly motivates adding purchase-oriented outcomes (complaints and other purchase proxies) in the empirical analysis. This is a meaningful framing/measurement justification, even though Rao does not reuse Manchanda et al.'s modeling approach.
relation_description: Rao (2022) draws on Manchanda et al. (2006) as empirical support that click behavior can diverge from purchase behavior, and uses that point to justify examining stronger purchase proxies beyond visits. The citation informs the paper's measurement framing rather than being directly extended methodologically.
